steps.text: Remove gloves and other PPE in a manner that minimizes personal contamination and transfer of infectious materials outside of the areas where infectious materials and/or animals are housed or manipulated.

Ensure that eating, drinking, smoking, handling contact lenses, applying cosmetics, and storing food for human consumption are not permitted in laboratory areas. Store food outside the laboratory area.

constraints.text: Eating, drinking, smoking, handling contact lenses, applying cosmetics, and storing food for human consumption are not permitted in laboratory areas.

steps.text: Develop and implement policies for the safe handling of sharps.
Substitute plasticware for glassware whenever possible.
Limit the use of needles and syringes in the animal facility to situations where there is no alternative.
Perform uncapping of needles to reduce potential for needlestick.
Do not bend, shear, break, recap, or otherwise manipulate needles by hand before disposal.
Use a hands-free device or comparable safety procedure if necessary to remove a needle from a syringe or recap a needle.
Carefully place used, disposable needles and syringes in puncture-resistant containers for sharps disposal immediately after use.
Place non-disposable sharps in a hard-walled container for transport to a processing area for decontamination.
Remove broken glassware using a brush and dustpan, tongs, or forceps.
Perform all procedures to minimize the creation of aerosols or splatters of infectious materials and waste.
Decontaminate work surfaces after completion of work and after any spill or splash of potentially infectious material with appropriate disinfectant.
Decontaminate all cultures, stocks, and other potentially infectious materials.
Materials to be decontaminated outside of the immediate animal room are placed in a durable, leak-proof container and secured for transport.
For infectious materials, the outer surface of the container is disinfected prior to moving materials and the transport container has a universal biohazard label.
Materials to be removed from the facility for decontamination are packed in accordance with applicable local, state, and federal regulations.
An effective integrated pest management program is required.
Animals and plants not associated with the work being performed are not permitted in the areas where infectious materials and/or animals are housed or manipulated.

preconditions.text: Individuals with valvular heart disease should not work with C. burnetii.
Work with C. burnetii should be avoided during pregnancy.

constraints.text: Exposure to naturally infected, often asymptomatic, sheep and their birth products is a documented hazard to personnel.
Adherence to BSL-3 practices is essential.

steps.text: Ensure laboratory personnel and visitors have documented polio vaccination.
Administer another dose of IPV for persons at increased occupational risk.
Implement the containment measures specified in GAP III.
Destroy non-essential poliovirus materials in certified facilities.
Ensure BSL-2 and ABSL-2 practices are followed for activities with poliovirus.
Contact the U.S. NAC for enhanced measures for work with eradicated poliovirus types and strains.

constraints.text: Laboratories should work with attenuated Sabin OPV strains unless there are strong scientific reasons for working with wild polioviruses.
Entities that possess, use, store, or transfer BoNT-producing species of Clostridium are required to be registered with the Federal Select Agent Program (FSAP).
Domestic transfer or importation of BoNT-producing species of Clostridium or BoNT above the permissible amount require prior approval from FSAP.
A DoC permit may be required for the export of these agents and toxin to another country.
SE are highly toxic by intravenous and inhalation routes of exposure, with lethal doses causing death in NHPs mainly due to shock and/or pulmonary edema.
Exposure of mucous membranes to SEB in a laboratory setting or in clinical studies has been reported to cause conjunctivitis and localized cutaneous swelling.
Intradermal or dermal exposure to concentrated SE solutions or patch tests (≥ 1μg/cm2) has resulted in erythema, induration, or dermatitis.

steps.text: Open flames are neither required nor recommended in the near microbe-free environment of a biological safety cabinet.

When deemed absolutely necessary and approved by the appropriate facility authorities after a thorough risk assessment, touch-plate micro burners equipped with a pilot light to provide a flame on demand may be used.
The burner must be turned off when work is completed.
Aspirator bottles or suction flasks should be connected to an overflow collection flask containing appropriate disinfectant and to an in-line HEPA or equivalent filter.

Inactivation of aspirated materials can be accomplished by placing a volume of a chemical decontamination solution having a concentration of chemical sufficient to decontaminate microorganisms when the flask is filled to its maximum capacity into the flask to inactivate the microorganisms as they are collected.
Investigators must determine the appropriate method of decontaminating wastes that will be removed from the BSC at the conclusion of the work.
When chemical means alone are appropriate, a suitable liquid disinfectant should be placed into a discard pan before work begins.
Items should be introduced into the pan with minimum splatter, covered completely, and allowed appropriate contact time as per manufacturer’s instructions.
Alternatively, liquids can be autoclaved prior to disposal.
The liquid container should be placed in a suitable, secondary container, and the outside of these containers wiped with a suitable liquid disinfectant, prior to removal from the BSC.
When a steam autoclave is used for solid wastes, contaminated materials should be placed into a biohazard bag or discard pan.
The bag should be loosely closed (to allow steam to enter the bag) or the discard pan should be covered in the BSC prior to transfer to the autoclave.
Restrict access to containment areas to authorized personnel.
Secure all entry and exit points with locks or equivalent electronic access systems.
Protect entry and exit points with alarms that alert authorities of unauthorized movement.
Install a double door vestibule at the entrance to the ABSL-3Ag containment facility.
Ensure doors in the vestibule are mechanically interlocked to prevent simultaneous opening.
Train personnel to close APR doors completely without damaging them when entering or exiting.
Conduct a site-specific risk assessment for installing a second APR door between the animal room exit and the biocontainment facility exit.
Ensure that the containment area is maintained under negative pressure when the facility-level APR door is opened.
Equip APR doors with pneumatic or mechanical compression seals as necessary.
Check and adjust mechanical compression seals at regular intervals.
Ensure pneumatic lines for APR doors are equipped with HEPA filters and check valves.
Seal and verify integral features of all APR doors through pressure decay testing.
Conduct pressure decay testing before occupying the facility, after structural modifications, and at regular intervals determined by a risk assessment.
Reinforce APR doors as necessary to ensure the integrity of the door seal.
Conduct a factory acceptance test on APR doors to simulate anticipated impact load.

constraints.text: Bioseal dampers must consistently fail in the closed position and continue to function properly during power failures.
Gaskets should be constructed of materials that will seal properly, regardless of scheduled applications of lubricants and/or sealants.
Pressure decay testing must be used to confirm that HEPA filter frames, housing, and the ductwork are airtight.
Methods for effectively decontaminating sections of potentially contaminated ductwork that extends outside the ABSL-3Ag facility should be identified and validated.

Precautions should be taken if fast-acting dampers are used in closed rooms instead of redundant supply fans because extreme negative pressures can develop that can injure personnel and animals or cause structural damage.
Used pre-filters should be regarded as contaminated and disinfected or decontaminated by a validated method before they are removed from the ABSL-3Ag facility for disposal.
Plumbing traps must be kept filled with liquid disinfectant or capped, and the atmospheric vents must have HEPA filters or their equivalent installed.

steps.text: Register entities that possess, use, or transfer Select Agents and Toxins with FSAP.
Approval must be obtained from FSAP for all individuals who have access to Select Agents and Toxins after a security risk assessment (SRA) by FBI.
Take enforcement actions for regulatory violations to address present risks and increase future compliance through administrative actions and/or civil monetary penalties.
Designate a Responsible Official (RO) for each registered entity to ensure compliance with Select Agent regulations.
Develop and implement a written security plan to safeguard Select Agents and/or Toxins against unauthorized access.
Develop and implement a written biosafety plan commensurate with the risk of Select Agents and/or Toxins.
Receive pre-approval for Restricted experiments that pose heightened safety and security risks.
Develop and implement a written incident response plan specific to hazards associated with Select Agents and/or Toxins.
Provide information and training on biosafety, security, and incident response to individuals with access to Select Agents and Toxins.

constraints.text: Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) reduces exposure by blocking the clinical laboratorian’s portals of entry.
Respiratory protection could be used when working with an infectious agent that is known or suspected to be airborne transmissible.
Working with an infectious agent that is known to be transmissible by blood may warrant protection of a person’s mucous membranes.
